1.1: There was a man in the land of Uz whose name was Job. This man was blameless and upright, one who feared God and turned away from evil.
1.2: He had seven sons and three daughters.
1.3: His possessions included seven thousand sheep, three thousand camels, five hundred yoke of oxen, and five hundred female donkeys, together with a very large household of servants. He was the greatest man among all the people of the east.
1.4: His sons would take turns holding feasts in their homes, each on his appointed day, and they would invite their three sisters to eat and drink with them.
1.5: When each round of feasting was over, Job would send for his children and consecrate them. He would rise early in the morning and offer a burnt offering for each of them all, for he said, 'Perhaps my sons have sinned and cursed God in their hearts.' This is what Job did regularly.
1.6: One day the heavenly beings came to present themselves before the LORD, and the Adversary came among them as well.
1.7: The LORD said to the Adversary, 'Where have you come from?' The Adversary answered the LORD, 'From roaming through the earth and walking back and forth on it.'
1.8: The LORD said to the Adversary, 'Have you taken notice of my servant Job? There is no one like him on earth — blameless and upright, fearing God and turning from evil.'
1.9: The Adversary answered the LORD, 'Does Job fear God for no reason?'
1.10: 'Have you not put a protective fence around him and his household and all he has on every side? You have blessed the work of his hands, and his wealth has multiplied throughout the land.'
1.11: 'But stretch out your hand and strike everything he has — he will surely curse you to your face.'
1.12: The LORD said to the Adversary, 'Very well, everything he has is in your power. Only do not lay a hand on the man himself.' So the Adversary departed from the LORD's presence.
1.13: One day his sons and daughters were eating and drinking wine at the oldest brother's house.
1.14: A messenger came running to Job and said, 'The oxen were plowing and the donkeys were grazing nearby —'
1.15: '— the Sabeans raided us. They seized the livestock and cut down the servants with the sword. I alone escaped to tell you!'
1.16: While that man was still speaking, another arrived: 'Fire from God fell from heaven and burned up the sheep and all the servants, consuming them. I alone escaped to tell you!'
1.17: While that man was still speaking, another arrived: 'Three Chaldean raiding parties swept in, took all the camels, and killed the servants with the sword. I alone escaped to tell you!'
1.18: While that man was still speaking, another arrived: 'Your sons and daughters were eating and drinking at the oldest brother's house —'
1.19: '— a great wind came out of the wilderness and struck the four corners of the house. It collapsed on the young people, and they are all dead. I alone escaped to tell you!'
1.20: At this, Job stood up, tore his outer garment, shaved his head, fell to the ground, and worshipped.
1.21: He said, 'Naked I came from my mother's womb, and naked I will return. The LORD gave, and the LORD has taken away; may the name of the LORD be praised.'
1.22: In all this, Job did not sin or accuse God of wrongdoing.

2.1: Again the heavenly beings came to present themselves before the LORD, and the Adversary came among them as well to stand before the LORD.
2.2: The LORD said to the Adversary, 'Where have you come from?' The Adversary answered, 'From roaming through the earth and walking back and forth on it.'
2.3: The LORD said to the Adversary, 'Have you taken note of my servant Job? There is no one like him on earth — blameless and upright, fearing God and turning from evil. He still maintains his integrity, even though you incited me against him to ruin him without cause.'
2.4: The Adversary answered the LORD, 'Skin for skin! A man will give everything he has to save his own life.'
2.5: 'But reach out now and strike his flesh and his bones — he will surely curse you to your face.'
2.6: The LORD said to the Adversary, 'Very well, he is in your hands. Only spare his life.'
2.7: The Adversary left the LORD's presence and afflicted Job with painful, festering sores from the soles of his feet to the top of his head.
2.8: Job took a piece of broken pottery to scrape his sores while he sat among the ashes.
2.9: His wife said to him, 'Are you still holding on to your integrity? Curse God and be done with it.'
2.10: But he replied, 'You are speaking like a foolish woman. Shall we accept good from God and refuse to accept adversity?' In all this, Job did not sin with his lips.
2.11: When Job's three friends — Eliphaz the Temanite, Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite — heard about all the adversity that had come upon him, they each came from their own region and arranged to meet together to express their sympathy and comfort him.
2.12: When they looked up from a distance, they could not even recognize him. They wept aloud, each tearing his outer garment and throwing dust into the air over his head as a gesture of grief directed toward heaven.
2.13: They sat on the ground beside him for seven days and seven nights. No one spoke a single word to him, for they could see how overwhelming his suffering was.

3.1: After this, Job opened his mouth and cursed the day he was born.
3.3: Let the day on which I was born perish, and the night that announced, 'A boy has been conceived.'
3.4: Let that day be turned to darkness. May God above not care for it, nor light shine upon it.
3.5: May darkness and deep shadow reclaim it; may a cloud settle over it; may the blackness of that day strike it with terror.
3.6: As for that night — let pitch darkness take it; let it not be counted among the days of the year; let it not enter the reckoning of the months.
3.7: Let that night be barren; let no sound of joy enter it.
3.8: Let those who are skilled at cursing days put a curse on it — those who know how to rouse Leviathan.
3.9: May the stars of its predawn twilight grow dark; may it wait for light that never comes; may it never see the breaking of morning.
3.10: Because it did not close the doors of my mother's womb to me, nor hide trouble from my eyes.
3.11: Why did I not die at birth? Why did I not perish when I came from the womb?
3.12: Why were there knees to receive me, and breasts for me to nurse?
3.13: For then I would have lain down and been at rest; I would have slept and been at peace —
3.14: with kings and counselors of the earth who built themselves ruins,
3.15: or with princes who hoarded gold and filled their mansions with silver.
3.16: Or why was I not like a stillborn hidden away, like infants who never saw the light of day?
3.17: There the wicked cease from their turmoil, and there the exhausted find rest.
3.18: There the prisoners rest together; they no longer hear the taskmaster's shout.
3.19: The lowly and the great are alike there, and the slave is free from his master.
3.20: Why is light given to the one who suffers, and life to those whose souls are bitter?
3.21: to those who long for death that does not come, who search for it more eagerly than for buried treasure,
3.22: who would be overjoyed and exult when they finally reach the grave?
3.23: Why is life given to a man whose way is hidden, whom God has fenced in on every side?
3.24: For my groaning comes even before my food, and my cries pour out like rushing water.
3.25: What I dreaded most has befallen me; what I feared has come upon me.
3.26: I had no ease, no rest, no peace — yet trouble still came.

4.2: If someone ventures a word with you, will it trouble you? Yet who can hold back from speaking?
4.3: Look — you have instructed many people, and you have strengthened weak hands.
4.4: Your words have upheld the one who was stumbling, and you have steadied feeble knees.
4.5: But now that it has come upon you, you are impatient; it has touched you, and you are terrified.
4.6: Is not your reverence for God your confidence, and the integrity of your ways your hope?
4.7: Think: has any innocent person ever perished? Have the upright ever been destroyed?
4.8: As I have observed, those who plow trouble and sow wickedness reap the same.
4.9: By the breath of God they perish, and by the blast of his nostrils they are consumed.
4.10: The roar of the lion, the voice of the fierce lion — the teeth of the young lions are shattered.
4.11: The mature lion perishes for lack of prey, and the cubs of the lioness scatter.
4.12: A word came to me in secret, and my ear caught a faint whisper of it.
4.13: In disturbing thoughts from visions of the night, when deep sleep descends on people,
4.14: fear came over me and trembling, making all my bones shake.
4.15: A spirit passed before my face; the hair on my body bristled.
4.16: It stopped still, but I could not make out its form. A shape was before my eyes; silence — and then a voice:
4.17: 'Can a mortal be more righteous than God? Can a person be purer than their Maker?'
4.18: 'God places no trust even in his servants; he charges his very angels with error.'
4.19: 'How much more those who live in houses of clay, whose foundation is the dust, who are crushed more easily than a moth!'
4.20: 'Between morning and evening they are destroyed; without anyone noticing, they vanish forever.'
4.21: 'Is not their tent stake pulled up from within them? They die, and that without gaining wisdom.'

5.1: Call out now — is there anyone who will answer you? To which of the holy ones will you appeal?
5.2: Vexation kills the foolish, and jealousy destroys the naive.
5.3: I have seen the fool put down roots, only to see his household suddenly cursed.
5.4: His children are kept from safety; they are crushed at the city gate, with no one to rescue them.
5.5: The hungry eat up his harvest, snatching it from among the thorns; they swallow up everything he owned.
5.6: Affliction does not rise from the dust, nor does trouble spring from the ground —
5.7: but people are born to trouble as naturally as sparks fly upward.
5.8: As for me, I would seek God, and to God I would commit my cause —
5.9: who does great and unsearchable things, marvellous things beyond counting —
5.10: who sends rain upon the earth and water upon the open fields;
5.11: who lifts up the lowly on high, and raises those in mourning to safety;
5.12: who thwarts the schemes of the cunning, so that their hands achieve nothing of what they plan;
5.13: he traps the wise in their own shrewdness, and the schemes of the crafty are swept away.
5.14: They encounter darkness in broad daylight and grope at midday as though it were night.
5.15: But he saves the poor from the cutting words of their enemies and from the grip of the powerful.
5.16: So the poor find hope, and injustice is silenced.
5.17: See, blessed is the person whom God reproves; do not despise the discipline of the Almighty.
5.18: For he wounds and then bandages; he strikes down and his own hands heal.
5.19: From six troubles he will deliver you; in seven, no evil will reach you.
5.20: In famine he will redeem you from death, and in war from the lethal edge of the sword.
5.21: You will be sheltered from the lash of the tongue, and will not dread when disaster comes.
5.22: You will laugh at destruction and famine, and will not dread the wild animals of the land.
5.23: You will have a pact with the stones of the field, and the wild animals will be at peace with you.
5.24: You will know that your home is secure, and when you check your flocks, nothing will be missing.
5.25: You will know that your children will be numerous, and your descendants like the grass of the earth.
5.26: You will come to your grave in ripe old age, like a sheaf of grain harvested in its proper season.
5.27: Look, we have examined this carefully; it is true. Hear it and take it to heart.

6.2: If only my grief could be weighed, and all my calamity placed on the scales!
6.3: It would be heavier than the sand of the sea; no wonder my words have been rash.
6.4: The Almighty's arrows are lodged in me; my spirit is drinking their venom; the terrors of God are lined up against me.
6.5: Does a wild donkey bray when it has grass? Does an ox bellow when it has its fodder?
6.6: Can bland food be eaten without salt? Is there any taste in the white of an egg?
6.7: My appetite refuses to touch such things; they are like loathsome food to me.
6.8: If only I could have what I ask for, that God would grant my longing —
6.9: that God would be willing to crush me, to let loose his hand and cut me off!
6.10: This would be my consolation — to exult even in pain without mercy — because I have not concealed the words of the Holy One.
6.11: What is my strength, that I should hold on? What do I have to look forward to, that I should endure?
6.12: Is my strength like the strength of stone? Is my flesh made of bronze?
6.13: Is there no help left in me? Has all sound judgment been driven away?
6.14: The despairing person deserves steadfast love from a friend; to withhold it is to forsake the fear of the Almighty.
6.15: My brothers are as treacherous as a seasonal brook, like watercourses that flow away —
6.16: which run dark with melting ice, swollen where snow lies hidden;
6.17: but when the heat comes they disappear; when summer arrives, they dry up from their channel.
6.18: The courses they ran turn aside; they rise into wasteland and vanish.
6.19: The caravans of Tema looked for water; the travelers of Sheba hoped for it.
6.20: They are put to shame because they trusted them; they arrive and find themselves disappointed.
6.21: You have now become just like them — you see my disaster and shrink back in fear.
6.22: Have I asked you for a gift? Have I asked you to pay a bribe on my behalf out of your own money?
6.23: Or, 'Save me from my enemy's hand,' or, 'Rescue me from the grip of the ruthless'?
6.24: Teach me, and I will be quiet; show me in what way I have gone wrong.
6.25: How compelling honest words are! But what does your argumentation actually prove?
6.26: Do you intend to reprove words, as if the speech of a despairing man were just wind?
6.27: You would cast lots over an orphan and sell out your friend.
6.28: Now then, be willing to look me in the face, for I will not lie to you.
6.29: Come back now; let there be no injustice here. Come back again — my integrity is at stake.
6.30: Is there any wrongdoing on my tongue? Can my own sense of taste not tell what is harmful?

7.1: Does not man have hard service on earth, and are not his days like those of a hired worker?
7.2: Like a servant longing for the shade, like a worker waiting eagerly for his pay —
7.3: So I am allotted months of futility, and wearisome nights are assigned to me.
7.4: When I lie down I say, 'When will I get up?' But the night stretches long and I toss about until dawn.
7.5: My flesh is covered with worms and a crust of earth; my skin cracks and oozes.
7.6: My days are swifter than a weaver's shuttle, and they end without hope.
7.7: Remember that my life is but a breath; my eyes will never again see happiness.
7.8: The eye of whoever sees me now will see me no more; you will look for me, but I will be gone.
7.9: As a cloud vanishes and is gone, so the one who descends to Sheol does not come up.
7.10: He will never return home, and his place will know him no more.
7.11: Therefore I will not hold back; I will speak in the anguish of my spirit and complain in the bitterness of my soul.
7.12: Am I the sea, or a sea monster, that you place a guard over me?
7.13: When I say, 'My bed will comfort me; my couch will ease my complaint' —
7.14: — but then you terrify me with dreams and frighten me with visions,
7.15: so that I would choose strangulation and death rather than this suffering in my body.
7.16: I am through with it; I would not live forever. Leave me be, for my days are only a breath.
7.17: What is man, that you should make so much of him, and that you fix your attention on him —
7.18: — testing him every morning, examining him every moment?
7.19: How long will you not look away from me, or leave me alone long enough to swallow?
7.20: If I have sinned, what have I done to you, O watcher of mankind? Why have you made me your target, till I am a burden to myself?
7.21: Why do you not pardon my offense and forgive my sin? For soon I shall lie down in the dust; you will search for me diligently, but I will be gone.

8.1: Then Bildad the Shuhite answered and said:
8.2: How long will you say such things? The words of your mouth are nothing but a mighty blast of wind.
8.3: Does God distort justice? Does the Almighty corrupt what is right?
8.4: If your children sinned against him, he gave them over to the consequences of their own sin.
8.5: If you will seek God earnestly and plead with the Almighty for grace —
8.6: if you are pure and upright, surely then he will act on your behalf and restore your righteous home —
8.7: Though your beginnings were humble, your latter years will be far greater.
8.8: Ask the former generations and give heed to the wisdom our ancestors have searched out —
8.9: for we are but of yesterday and know nothing; our days on earth are but a shadow.
8.10: Will they not teach you, tell you, and speak from the depths of their understanding?
8.11: Can papyrus grow where there is no marsh? Can reeds flourish without water?
8.12: Though it is still green and not yet cut, it withers before any other herb.
8.13: Such is the end of all who forget God; the hope of the godless will come to nothing.
8.14: What he trusts in will be cut off, and his confidence is like a spider's web.
8.15: He leans against his house, but it does not stand; he clings to it, but it will not hold.
8.16: He is well watered in the sunshine, his shoots spreading over the garden.
8.17: His roots twine around the pile of stones; he forces his way into a stony place.
8.18: But if he is destroyed from his place, it says to him: 'I have never seen you.'
8.19: Behold, such is the joy of his path; and from the earth others will spring up in his place.
8.20: God does not reject the blameless, nor does he take hold of the hand of wrongdoers.
8.21: He will yet fill your mouth with laughter and your lips with joyful shouts.
8.22: Your enemies will be clothed with shame, and the tent of the wicked will vanish.

9.2: Indeed, I know this is true. But how can a man be justified before God?
9.3: If one wished to dispute with him, one could not answer him once in a thousand attempts.
9.4: God is wise in heart and mighty in strength — who has defied him and survived intact?
9.5: He removes mountains before they know it, overturning them in his anger —
9.6: who shakes the earth from its place so that its pillars quake —
9.7: who commands the sun not to rise, and seals up the stars —
9.8: who alone stretched out the heavens and walks on the crests of the sea —
9.9: who made the Bear, Orion, and the Pleiades, and the constellations of the southern sky —
9.10: who does great things beyond comprehension and marvelous things without number.
9.11: When he passes by me, I cannot see him; when he moves on, I cannot perceive him.
9.12: If he snatches something away, who can stop him? Who will dare say to him, 'What are you doing?'
9.13: God does not restrain his anger; even the allies of Rahab crouched beneath him.
9.14: How then can I answer him, or select my words to argue before him?
9.15: Even if I were righteous, I could not answer him; I would have to plead for mercy to my own judge.
9.16: If I summoned him and he responded, I still could not believe he was truly listening to me.
9.17: For he would crush me with a storm and multiply my wounds for no reason.
9.18: He does not let me catch my breath, but fills me with bitter suffering.
9.19: If it is a contest of strength, he is the stronger; if it is a matter of justice, who can summon him to court?
9.20: If I justified myself, my own mouth would condemn me; if I declared myself blameless, he would prove me twisted.
9.21: Though I am blameless, I do not trust my own self-knowledge; I despise my own life.
9.22: It is all the same to me; this is why I say: he destroys the blameless and the wicked alike.
9.23: When sudden disaster brings death, he mocks at the calamity of the innocent.
9.24: The earth is given into the power of the wicked; he blindfolds its judges. If not he, then who?
9.25: My days are swifter than a runner; they flee away, seeing no good.
9.26: They glide away like skiffs of reed, like an eagle diving on its prey.
9.27: If I say, 'I will forget my complaint, I will put on a cheerful face and be bright' —
9.28: I dread all my suffering, for I know you will not acquit me.
9.29: If I am condemned already, why do I exhaust myself for nothing?
9.30: Even if I washed myself with snow water and cleansed my hands with soap —
9.31: — you would still plunge me into a slime pit so that my own clothes would recoil from me.
9.32: For he is not a man as I am, that I could answer him or that we could appear in court together.
9.33: There is no umpire between us who could place his hand upon us both.
9.34: Let him remove his rod from me, and let his terror not overwhelm me.
9.35: Then I would speak without fear, for I am not so in myself.

10.1: My soul is sick of my life; I will give free rein to my complaint; I will speak in the bitterness of my soul.
10.2: I will say to God: Do not condemn me; let me know why you bring charges against me.
10.3: Does it please you to oppress, to reject the work of your own hands, while you smile on the plans of the wicked?
10.4: Do you have eyes of flesh? Do you see as a human being sees?
10.5: Are your days like those of a human being, or your years like a man's years?
10.6: that you search out my wrongdoing and inquire into my sin —
10.7: though you know I am not guilty, and no one can rescue me from your power?
10.8: Your hands shaped and formed me — the whole of me — and now you turn and destroy me.
10.9: Remember that you formed me like clay; will you now bring me back to dust?
10.10: Did you not pour me out like milk and curdle me like cheese?
10.11: You clothed me with skin and flesh, and knit me together with bones and sinews.
10.12: You gave me life and steadfast love, and your watchful care preserved my spirit.
10.13: Yet these things you hid in your heart; I know that this was your purpose all along.
10.14: If I sin, you take note of it and will not let my guilt go.
10.15: If I am wicked, woe to me! And if I am righteous, I still cannot hold my head up, for I am filled with shame and sunk in my affliction.
10.16: But if I do hold my head up, you hunt me like a fierce lion and again demonstrate your awesome power against me.
10.17: You bring fresh witnesses against me and increase your anger toward me; wave after wave of trouble and a host of hardships come against me.
10.18: Why then did you bring me out of the womb? I wish I had died and no eye had ever seen me!
10.19: I should never have existed; I should have been carried straight from the womb to the grave.
10.20: Are my days not few? Cease — leave me alone, that I may have a little relief before I go —
10.21: — to the land of darkness and the shadow of death, from which there is no return —
10.22: — a land of utter gloom like thick darkness, of the shadow of death and disorder, where even the light is as darkness.

11.1: Then Zophar the Naamathite answered and said:
11.2: Should this flood of words go unanswered? Should a man of endless talk be counted righteous?
11.3: Shall your boasting silence everyone? When you scoff, will no one rebuke you?
11.4: For you have said, 'My doctrine is right and I am clean in God's sight.'
11.5: If only God would speak and open his lips to answer you —
11.6: and reveal to you the secrets of wisdom — for wisdom has more facets than you know. Know this: God has required of you less than your sin deserves.
11.7: Can you search out the deep things of God? Can you find the limit of the Almighty?
11.8: It is higher than the heavens — what can you do? Deeper than Sheol — what can you know?
11.9: Its measure is longer than the earth and broader than the sea.
11.10: If he sweeps through, imprisons, and convenes a court — who can stop him?
11.11: For he knows worthless people; when he sees iniquity, does he not take note of it?
11.12: A hollow-headed man will become wise when a wild donkey gives birth to a human being.
11.13: If you would prepare your heart and stretch out your hands toward him —
11.14: if there is sin in your hand, remove it far away, and let wickedness have no place in your home —
11.15: then surely you will lift up your face without shame; you will stand firm and have no fear.
11.16: For you will forget your suffering; you will remember it only as water that has long since flowed on.
11.17: Your life will shine brighter than noon; even your darkest time will be like dawn.
11.18: You will have confidence, because there is hope; you will look around you and rest in security.
11.19: You will lie down, and no one will make you afraid; many will come seeking your goodwill.
11.20: But the eyes of the wicked will fail; all means of escape will be closed to them, and their only hope is to breathe their last.

12.2: No doubt you are the ones who know, and wisdom itself will die when you do.
12.3: But I have a mind as well as you; I am not less than you. Who does not know such things?
12.4: I am a laughingstock to my friends — I who called to God and he answered me — the just and blameless man is held in contempt.
12.5: In the mind of one who is comfortable, the lamp of the afflicted is despised — he stands ready to condemn those whose feet stumble.
12.6: The tents of robbers are undisturbed, and those who provoke God live in safety — God himself puts things into their hand.
12.7: Ask the animals and they will teach you; the birds of the air will tell you.
12.8: Or speak to the earth and it will teach you; the fish of the sea will declare it to you.
12.9: Who among all these does not know that the hand of the LORD has done this?
12.10: In his hand is the life of every living creature and the breath of all human flesh.
12.11: Does not the ear test words as the tongue tastes food?
12.12: Wisdom belongs to the aged, and understanding comes with long years.
12.13: With God are wisdom and strength; he has counsel and understanding.
12.14: What he tears down cannot be rebuilt; if he imprisons a man, there is no release.
12.15: If he holds back the waters, they dry up; if he releases them, they flood the land.
12.16: With him are strength and effective wisdom; those who deceive and those who are deceived both belong to him.
12.17: He leads counselors away stripped of dignity and turns judges into fools.
12.18: He strips kings of their authority and wraps a servant's cloth around their loins.
12.19: He leads priests away stripped and overturns those who seemed unassailable.
12.20: He silences those who are trusted and strips judgment from the aged.
12.21: He pours contempt on princes and loosens the strength of the mighty.
12.22: He brings to light the deep things hidden in darkness and brings the shadow of death into the open.
12.23: He makes nations great, then destroys them; he expands them, then scatters them.
12.24: He strips understanding from the leaders of the peoples of the earth and makes them wander in a trackless wilderness.
12.25: They grope in the dark without light, and he makes them stagger like drunkards.

13.1: My eye has seen all of this; my ear has heard and understood it.
13.2: What you know, I know as well — I am not less than you.
13.3: I want to speak directly to the Almighty; I long to argue my case before God.
13.4: But you — you are fabricators of lies; you are doctors who prescribe nothing but worthless remedies.
13.5: If only you would keep completely silent — that itself would be wisdom on your part.
13.6: Hear my argument now, and listen carefully to the pleadings of my lips.
13.7: Will you speak falsehood on God's behalf? Will you tell lies for his sake?
13.8: Will you plead his case with favoritism? Will you act as God's lawyer?
13.9: Will it go well when he examines you? Will you deceive him as one deceives a mortal man?
13.10: He will certainly call you out if you show favoritism in secret.
13.11: Will not his majesty strike terror into you, and his dread overwhelm you?
13.12: Your wise maxims are proverbs of ash; your arguments are arguments of clay.
13.13: Be silent and leave me alone so I can speak; let whatever comes, come.
13.14: Why do I risk my flesh in my teeth and put my life in my own hands?
13.15: Though he kills me, yet I will hope in him — but I will defend my own conduct before him.
13.16: This itself will be my deliverance — no godless man can stand in his presence.
13.17: Listen carefully to my words; let my declaration reach your ears.
13.18: Look — I have laid out my case; I know that I will be vindicated.
13.19: Who is there who will bring a charge against me? For if there is one, I will be silent and die.
13.20: Only do not do these two things to me, and I will not hide from your presence.
13.21: Take your hand off me, and do not let terror of you overwhelm me.
13.22: Then call, and I will answer; or let me speak first, and you answer me.
13.23: How many are my iniquities and sins? Tell me my transgression and my sin.
13.24: Why do you hide your face from me and treat me as your enemy?
13.25: Will you pursue a leaf tossed in the wind? Will you hunt down dry stubble?
13.26: You write bitter charges against me and saddle me with the sins of my youth.
13.27: You put my feet in stocks, you keep watch over all my paths, you mark the very prints of my feet.
13.28: And I waste away like something rotten, like a garment eaten through by moths.

14.1: Man born of woman is short-lived and full of turmoil.
14.2: He comes up like a flower and then wilts; he passes like a shadow and does not endure.
14.3: Yet on such a creature do you fix your eyes, and bring him into court before you?
14.4: Who can bring something pure from something impure? No one can.
14.5: Since his days are fixed, the number of his months determined by you, the limit set that he cannot cross —
14.6: — look away from him so he can rest, until he has completed his time like a hired worker.
14.7: For there is hope for a tree: if it is cut down, it will sprout again, and its new growth will not stop.
14.8: Though its root grows old in the earth and its stump dies in the soil —
14.9: — yet at the smell of water it will bud and put out branches like a new plant.
14.10: But man dies and lies helpless; he breathes his last — and where is he?
14.11: As water vanishes from a lake, and a river wastes away and dries up —
14.12: — so man lies down and does not rise; until the heavens are no more, they will not wake or be roused from their sleep.
14.13: O that you would hide me in Sheol, shelter me until your anger passes, set a fixed time for me, and then remember me!
14.14: If a man dies, will he live again? Through all my days of hard service I will wait until my release comes.
14.15: You will call out to me, and I will answer; you will long for the work of your hands.
14.16: But now you count my every step and watch over my sin without fail.
14.17: My transgression is sealed in a bag, and you have stitched up my guilt.
14.18: But a mountain eroding comes to nothing, and the rock is dislodged from its place;
14.19: water wears down the stones; floods wash away the soil of the earth — and so you wash away the hope of man.
14.20: You overpower him completely and he is gone; you alter his countenance and send him away.
14.21: His sons rise to honor, but he does not know it; they are brought low, but he is not aware.
14.22: He feels only the pain within him and mourns only over himself.

15.2: Should a wise man answer with empty words, filling himself with the hot east wind?
15.3: Should he argue with worthless words, with speeches that accomplish nothing?
15.4: You are actually undermining the fear of God and hindering prayer before him.
15.5: Your mouth reveals your own guilt; you have adopted the tongue of the cunning.
15.6: Your own mouth condemns you, not I; your very lips bear witness against you.
15.7: Are you the first man who was ever born? Were you shaped before the hills?
15.8: Have you been present in God's secret council? Do you think wisdom belongs to you alone?
15.9: What do you know that we do not? What insight have you that we lack?
15.10: Among us are both the gray-haired and the very aged, men far older than your father.
15.11: Are God's consolations not enough for you — the gentle word he has offered you?
15.12: Why does your heart carry you away? Why do your eyes flare —
15.13: — that you turn your spirit against God and pour out such words from your mouth?
15.14: What is a mortal man, that he could be clean? Can one born of woman be righteous?
15.15: God does not even trust his holy ones; the heavens themselves are not clean in his sight.
15.16: How much more abominable and vile is man, who laps up wickedness like water!
15.17: I will show you — listen to me; I will declare what I have seen —
15.18: — what wise men have declared, passed down from their fathers without concealment,
15.19: to those whose land was their own alone, before foreigners came among them —
15.20: The wicked man twists in anguish all his days; the years allotted to the tyrant are numbered.
15.21: A sound of terror is always in his ears; in his moments of peace the destroyer strikes him.
15.22: He has no hope of escaping from the darkness; he is marked for the sword.
15.23: He wanders in search of food, crying, 'Where is it?' He knows the day of darkness is ready and near.
15.24: Distress and anguish terrorize him; they overpower him like a king advancing to battle.
15.25: For he has stretched out his hand against God and defied the Almighty,
15.26: charging at him neck-forward, pressing against the thick boss of his shield,
15.27: He has become fat-faced, his sides padded with flesh —
15.28: he makes his home in ruined cities, in houses no one else inhabits, houses on the verge of collapse —
15.29: He will not remain wealthy; his substance will not last; he will not extend his holdings over the earth.
15.30: He will not escape from the darkness; flame will wither his branches, and by the breath of God's mouth he will be swept away.
15.31: Let him not deceive himself by trusting in what is empty, for emptiness will be his only return.
15.32: It will be completed before his time; his branch will not stay green.
15.33: He will shed his unripe grapes like the vine, and drop his blossoms like the olive tree.
15.34: For the assembly of the godless will be desolate, and fire will consume the tents of corruption.
15.35: They conceive trouble and give birth to evil; their inmost being produces only deceit.

16.2: I have heard all this before. You are miserable comforters, every one of you.
16.3: Will these empty words never stop? What gives you the confidence to keep answering me?
16.4: I could talk just like you if you were in my place — I could pile words against you and shake my head at you.
16.5: I would strengthen you with my words, and the comfort of my lips would ease your grief.
16.6: If I speak, the pain is not relieved; and if I stay silent, how much of it leaves me?
16.7: But now he has utterly exhausted me; he has made desolate everyone around me.
16.8: You have wasted my flesh until it shows — that itself is a witness; my emaciation rises up and testifies against me to my face.
16.9: He has torn me apart in his wrath and pursued me; he gnashes his teeth at me; my enemy fixes his sharpened gaze upon me.
16.10: They open their mouths wide at me; they strike my cheek in contempt; they have gathered themselves together against me.
16.11: God has surrendered me to the evildoers and thrown me into the hands of the wicked.
16.12: I was living in ease, and he shattered me; he grabbed me by the neck and broke me apart; he stood me up as his target.
16.13: His archers surround me; he slashes into my kidneys without mercy and spills my gall on the ground.
16.14: He breaks through me, breach after breach; he charges at me like a great warrior.
16.15: I have sewn sackcloth over my skin and pressed my dignity down into the dust.
16.16: My face is flushed raw from weeping, and the shadow of death rests on my eyelids —
16.17: — yet no violence has been done by my hands, and my prayer has been pure.
16.18: O earth, do not cover my blood; let my cry find no place to settle!
16.19: Even now my witness is in heaven, and my advocate is on high.
16.20: My friends scorn me, yet my eye pours out tears to God.
16.21: If only a mortal man could plead with God the way a man pleads for his neighbor —
16.22: — for the few years left are slipping past, and I am walking a road from which there is no return.

17.1: My spirit is spent; my days are put out; the grave is waiting for me.
17.2: Surely there are mockers all around me, and my eye rests on their hostile provocation.
17.3: Give me a pledge yourself; who else is there to strike hands with me?
17.4: For you have closed off their minds from true understanding; therefore you will not let them triumph.
17.5: He who betrays his friends for personal gain — his children's eyes will fail.
17.6: He has turned me into a proverb among the nations — I am one before whose face men spit.
17.7: My eye has grown dim from sorrow, and all my limbs are like shadows.
17.8: Upright men are horrified by this; the innocent rises up against the godless.
17.9: The righteous man keeps to his course, and the one with clean hands grows stronger and stronger.
17.10: But come, all of you, try again — I still cannot find a wise man among you.
17.11: My days are over; my plans have been cut off — even the secret longings of my heart.
17.12: They make night into day; they say, 'The light is near,' even while darkness surrounds them.
17.13: If I wait, Sheol is my home — I have made my bed in the darkness.
17.14: I call out to corruption, 'You are my father,' and to the worm, 'My mother and my sister.'
17.15: Where then is my hope? Who can see hope for me?
17.16: Will it descend with me to the gates of Sheol? Together we shall go down into the dust.

18.2: How long before you stop talking? Think first, and then we will have our say.
18.3: Why do you regard us as animals? Why are we worthless in your eyes?
18.4: You who tear yourself apart in your rage — will the earth be forsaken on your account? Will the rock be moved from its place for you?
18.5: Yes, the light of the wicked is extinguished; the flame of his fire gives no light.
18.6: The light in his tent grows dark, and the lamp over him is extinguished.
18.7: His powerful stride is shortened, and his own schemes bring him down.
18.8: He is thrown into a net by his own feet, and he walks right onto a snare.
18.9: A trap catches him by the heel; a snare holds him tight.
18.10: A rope is buried in the ground for him, and a trap waits on his path.
18.11: Terrors frighten him on every side and push him forward at every step.
18.12: His strength is starved, and disaster stands ready at his side.
18.13: His skin is eaten away piece by piece; the firstborn of death devours his body.
18.14: His confidence is ripped away from his home, and he is brought before the king of terrors.
18.15: Something alien has moved into his tent; brimstone is scattered over his home.
18.16: His roots dry up below; his branches wither above.
18.17: His memory vanishes from the land; he has no name in the open places.
18.18: He is driven from light into darkness and banished from the world.
18.19: He has no offspring or descendants among his people, and no survivor where he once lived.
18.20: Those who come after him are horrified by his fate; those who went before him are seized with dread.
18.21: Surely such are the homes of the wicked, and this is the dwelling of the one who does not know God.

19.2: How long will you torment me and crush my soul with words?
19.3: Ten times now you have reproached me; you are not ashamed to wrong me.
19.4: Even if I have truly erred, my error rests with me alone.
19.5: If you truly exalt yourselves over me and press my disgrace against me as evidence,
19.6: then know that God has overthrown me and thrown his net around me.
19.7: I shout 'Injustice!' but no one answers; I call for justice but there is no verdict.
19.8: He has blocked my way so I cannot pass; he has set darkness over my paths.
19.9: He has stripped me of my honor and taken the crown from my head.
19.10: He has broken me down on every side and I am gone; he has pulled up my hope like a tree.
19.11: He has kindled his wrath against me and counted me as one of his enemies.
19.12: His troops advance together; they heap up siege ramps against me and encamp around my tent.
19.13: He has put my brothers far from me, and those who knew me have become wholly estranged.
19.14: My relatives have failed me, and my familiar friends have forgotten me.
19.15: Those living in my house and my female servants count me as a stranger; I am a foreigner in their eyes.
19.16: I called my servant, but he gave me no answer; I had to beg him with my own mouth.
19.17: My breath is repulsive to my wife; I am loathsome to the sons of my own body.
19.18: Even young children despise me; when I stand up they speak against me.
19.19: All my closest companions abhor me; those I loved have turned against me.
19.20: My bones cling to my skin and flesh; I have escaped with only the skin of my teeth.
19.21: Have pity on me, have pity on me, O my friends, for the hand of God has struck me.
19.22: Why do you pursue me like God, and are not satisfied with my flesh?
19.23: O that my words were written down! O that they were inscribed in a book!
19.24: That with an iron pen and lead they were cut into the rock forever!
19.25: For I know that my Redeemer lives, and that at the last he will stand upon the earth.
19.26: And after my skin is thus destroyed, yet from within my flesh I shall see God,
19.27: whom I myself shall see, and my own eyes shall behold — not a stranger's eyes. My heart fails within me with longing.
19.28: If you say, 'Why are we still pursuing him?' when the root of the trouble lies within him —
19.29: — then fear the sword, for wrath brings the sword's punishment, so that you may know there is indeed a judgment.

20.2: Therefore my thoughts push me to answer; I feel urgency within me.
20.3: I hear reproof that dishonors me, and a spirit from beyond my understanding compels me to respond.
20.4: Do you not know this from of old, since man was set upon the earth,
20.5: that the triumph of the wicked is short, and the joy of the godless lasts but a moment?
20.6: Though his greatness mounts to the heavens and his head reaches the clouds —
20.7: — he will perish forever like his own dung; those who saw him will say, 'Where is he?'
20.8: He flies away like a dream and cannot be found; he is chased off like a vision of the night.
20.9: The eye that saw him will see him no more; his own place will no longer recognize him.
20.10: His children will seek favor from the poor, and his hands will give back their wealth.
20.11: His bones are full of the sins of his youth; they will lie down with him in the dust.
20.12: Though evil is sweet in his mouth and he hides it under his tongue —
20.13: — though he spares it and will not let it go, but keeps it lodged in his mouth —
20.14: — yet his food turns in his stomach; it becomes cobra venom inside him.
20.15: He swallowed wealth down, and will vomit it up again; God will expel it from his belly.
20.16: He will suck the venom of cobras; the tongue of the viper will kill him.
20.17: He will not look on the rivers, the streams, the brooks flowing with honey and butter.
20.18: He must restore the fruits of his labor without consuming them; according to his wealth the restitution will be, and he will not rejoice in it.
20.19: For he oppressed and abandoned the poor; he seized a house he did not build.
20.20: He will not feel peace within; he will retain nothing of what he desired.
20.21: None of his food is left; therefore no one will inherit his property.
20.22: In the very fullness of his plenty he will be in distress; every hand of the wretched will come against him.
20.23: When he is set to fill his belly, God will hurl his fierce wrath upon him and rain it down on him as he eats.
20.24: He flees from the iron weapon, and the bronze bow strikes him through.
20.25: The arrow is drawn; it passes through his body; the shining blade comes out through his gall; terrors overwhelm him.
20.26: All darkness is kept in store for his treasures; a fire not fanned by wind shall consume him; disaster will fall on what remains in his tent.
20.27: The heavens will expose his guilt, and the earth will rise up against him.
20.28: The produce of his house will depart; his possessions will pour away in the day of his anger.
20.29: This is the portion of the wicked man from God — the heritage decreed for him by God.

21.2: Listen carefully to what I say; let this be your consolation.
21.3: Bear with me so I may speak; and after I have spoken, mock away.
21.4: As for me, is my complaint directed to man? Why then should I not be impatient?
21.5: Look at me and be appalled; lay your hand over your mouth.
21.6: Even when I think of it I am troubled, and shuddering seizes my flesh.
21.7: Why do the wicked go on living, grow old, and even grow mighty in power?
21.8: Their children are established around them, and their offspring before their eyes.
21.9: Their houses are safe from fear; the rod of God is not upon them.
21.10: Their bull breeds without fail; their cow calves and does not miscarry.
21.11: Their little ones run out like a flock, and their children dance.
21.12: They take up timbrel and harp and rejoice at the sound of the flute.
21.13: They spend their days in prosperity and go down to Sheol in an instant.
21.14: Yet they say to God, 'Leave us alone; we have no desire to know your ways.'
21.15: Who is the Almighty that we should serve him? What do we gain by praying to him?
21.16: Their prosperity is not in their own hand — the counsel of the wicked I keep far from me.
21.17: How often is the lamp of the wicked actually extinguished? How often does disaster come upon them? Does God distribute pain in his anger?
21.18: They are as straw before the wind, and chaff swept away by the tempest.
21.19: You say God stores up punishment for his children; God repays the man himself and he will know it.
21.20: Let his own eyes see his destruction; let him drink of the wrath of the Almighty.
21.21: For what pleasure does he take in his house after him, when the number of his months is severed?
21.22: Will anyone teach God knowledge? He is the one who judges those on high.
21.23: One person dies in full vigor, completely at ease and secure,
21.24: his sides full of milk and his bones saturated with marrow.
21.25: And another dies in bitterness of soul, having never tasted what is good.
21.26: They lie down together in the dust, and worms cover them both.
21.27: I know your thoughts and the schemes you wrongfully contrive against me.
21.28: For you say, 'Where is the house of the noble man? Where are the dwelling places of the wicked?'
21.29: Have you not asked those who travel the roads? Do you not know their signs?
21.30: That the wicked is spared on the day of disaster and delivered through to the day of wrath.
21.31: Who will tell him to his face what he has done? Who will repay him for his deeds?
21.32: Yet he is borne to the grave, and a vigil is kept over his tomb.
21.33: The clods of the valley lie sweetly upon him; every person follows after him, as numberless are those who went before.
21.34: How then do you comfort me with empty words, when nothing but falsehood remains in your answers?

25.2: Dominion and dread belong to him; he makes peace in his high places.
25.3: Can his armies be counted? Upon whom does his light not rise?
25.4: How can any mortal be declared righteous before God? How can anyone born of a woman be clean?
25.5: Even the moon gives no real light before him; the very stars are not pure in his sight.
25.6: How much less a mortal, who is a maggot, and a human being, who is a worm!

26.2: What help you have given to the powerless! What strength you have brought to the feeble arm!
26.3: What counsel you have given to one with no wisdom! How abundantly you have set out sound understanding!
26.4: Who are you addressing with these words? And whose spirit was speaking through you?
26.5: The shades of the dead tremble under the waters, even those who dwell beneath them.
26.6: Sheol is laid bare before him, and Abaddon has no covering.
26.7: He stretches out the north over the void and hangs the earth on nothing.
26.8: He wraps the waters in his thick clouds, and the cloud holds without bursting.
26.9: He screens the face of his throne and spreads his cloud over it.
26.10: He has drawn a circle on the face of the waters, marking the boundary between light and dark.
26.11: The pillars of heaven tremble and are stunned at his rebuke.
26.12: By his power he calmed the sea; by his understanding he crushed the sea dragon.
26.13: His Spirit made the heavens beautiful; his hand transfixed the twisting serpent.
26.14: These things are but the edges of his ways; how faint a whisper we catch of him! The thunder of his full power — who can comprehend it?

27.1: And Job again took up his discourse and said:
27.2: As God lives, who has denied me justice, and the Almighty, who has made me bitter —
27.3: For as long as my breath remains and God's breath is in my nostrils —
27.4: My lips will not speak wickedness, and my tongue will not utter deception.
27.5: God forbid that I should agree you are right; until I die I will not surrender my integrity.
27.6: I hold fast to my righteousness and will not release it; my heart does not condemn me for a single day I have lived.
27.7: Let my enemy be treated as the wicked, and whoever rises against me as the unrighteous.
27.8: For what hope does the godless man have, however much he gains, when God takes away his life?
27.9: Will God hear his cry when trouble comes upon him?
27.10: Does he take delight in the Almighty? Does he call upon God at all times?
27.11: I will instruct you about the hand of God; I will not hide what the Almighty has shown me.
27.12: Look — you yourselves have seen all this; why then have you become so thoroughly empty?
27.13: This is the lot God assigns to a wicked man, and the inheritance oppressors receive from the Almighty.
27.14: If his children multiply, it is only for the sword; his descendants will not have enough to eat.
27.15: His survivors will be buried by plague, and his widows will not weep for them.
27.16: Though he pile up silver like dust and lay up clothing as plentiful as clay —
27.17: He may store it all up, but the righteous man will wear it, and the innocent will share out the silver.
27.18: He builds his house as a moth builds its web, and like the temporary shelter a watchman puts up.
27.19: The rich man lies down, but is not gathered to his fathers; he opens his eyes, and is gone.
27.20: Terrors sweep over him like a flood; a storm carries him off in the night.
27.21: The east wind sweeps him away and he is gone; a storm flings him from his home.
27.22: For God hurls his power at him without mercy; the man would desperately flee from his hand.
27.23: Men clap their hands at him and hiss him away from his home.

28.1: Surely silver has its mine, and gold a place where it is refined.
28.2: Iron is taken from the earth, and copper is smelted from ore.
28.3: A miner sets a limit to the darkness and searches to its utmost depth for stone in deep shadow.
28.4: Far from where people live, a shaft is sunk; forgotten by travelers' feet, the miners dangle far from others and sway.
28.5: The earth produces bread from its surface, but underneath it is turned up as though by fire.
28.6: Its stones are the source of sapphires, and it holds flakes of gold.
28.7: A path no bird of prey knows, unseen by the eye of the falcon.
28.8: The lion's cubs have not walked it, nor the fierce lion passed over it.
28.9: He puts his hand to the flint; he uproots mountains from their foundations.
28.10: He cuts channels through the rocks; his eye takes in every precious thing.
28.11: He holds back the streams from seeping through, and brings hidden things out to the light.
28.12: But where shall wisdom be found? And where is the place of understanding?
28.13: No mortal knows the path to it, and it is not found in the land of the living.
28.14: The deep says, 'It is not in me,' and the sea says, 'It is not with me.'
28.15: It cannot be bought with gold, and its price cannot be paid in silver.
28.16: It cannot be matched against the gold of Ophir, the precious onyx, or the sapphire.
28.17: Gold and crystal cannot match its worth; it cannot be exchanged for any item of fine gold.
28.18: Coral and crystal are not even worth mentioning; the value of wisdom is above rubies.
28.19: The topaz of Cush cannot compare with it; it cannot be priced against pure gold.
28.20: Where then does wisdom come from? And where is the place of understanding?
28.21: It is hidden from the eyes of every living creature and kept from the sight of the birds of the sky.
28.22: Abaddon and Death say, 'We have heard only a rumor of it.'
28.23: God understands the way to it and knows its location.
28.24: For he looks to the ends of the earth and sees everything under all of heaven.
28.25: When he assigned a weight to the wind and measured the waters —
28.26: When he established a decree for the rain and a path for the lightning and thunder —
28.27: Then he saw it and made it known; he established it and fully searched it out.
28.28: And to mankind he said: The fear of the Lord is wisdom, and to turn from evil is understanding.

22.2: Can any human being be of use to God? Even the wise man's wisdom serves himself alone.
22.3: Does the Almighty take any pleasure in your being righteous? Does he gain anything from you walking without blame?
22.4: Is it because of your reverence for him that he calls you to account and puts you on trial?
22.5: Your wickedness is immense — surely — and your iniquities without limit.
22.6: You have seized collateral from your kinsmen for no reason, and stripped people of the very clothes on their backs.
22.7: You have not given the weary traveler water to drink; you have withheld bread from the starving.
22.8: But the powerful man possessed the land, and the man of high standing settled in it.
22.9: You have turned widows away empty-handed; you have broken the strength of the orphan.
22.10: That is why traps are all around you now, and sudden terror has seized you.
22.11: Darkness so dense you cannot see through it, and a flood of waters sweeping over you.
22.12: Is not God in the heights of heaven? See how high the stars tower above you.
22.13: And you say, 'What does God know? Can he see through the dense cloud to give judgment?'
22.14: Dense clouds screen him so he cannot see; he walks only along the circuit of the sky.
22.15: Will you persist in the old road that wicked people once traveled —
22.16: — those who were cut down before their time, whose foundation was swept away by a flood?
22.17: They were the ones who said to God, 'Leave us alone' — and 'What can the Almighty do to us?'
22.18: Yet he had filled their houses with abundance — though the thinking of the wicked is far from my own.
22.19: The righteous see their destruction and celebrate; the innocent deride them:
22.20: Surely our opponents are destroyed; what remained of them the fire has devoured.
22.21: Make peace with him now and be reconciled; in that way good will find you.
22.22: Accept his instruction from his own mouth; store his words in your heart.
22.23: If you return to the Almighty, you will be restored; banish iniquity from your household,
22.24: then treat your gold like ordinary dust, and your Ophir gold like stream-bed pebbles;
22.25: and the Almighty himself will be your gold, your abundance of pure silver.
22.26: Then you will take your delight in the Almighty, and lift your face toward God.
22.27: You will pray to him and he will answer; you will fulfill what you have vowed.
22.28: Whatever you determine will be settled; light will shine on all your paths.
22.29: When others are brought low, you will say, 'There is hope'; and he will save the one who bows his head.
22.30: He will rescue even the person who is not innocent; he will be delivered through the purity of your hands.

23.2: Even today my complaint is bitter; the burden laid on me is heavier than my crying out can express.
23.3: If only I knew where to find him — if only I could reach the place where he dwells!
23.4: I would present my case before him in full order, and fill my mouth with the strongest arguments.
23.5: I would know what he would say in reply, and understand every word of his answer.
23.6: Would he use his vast power to overwhelm me in argument? No — he would hear me out.
23.7: There a person of integrity could bring his case; and I would receive a final acquittal from my judge.
23.8: But I go east, and he is not there; I go west, and cannot find him;
23.9: he works on the left, and I cannot see him; he conceals himself on the right, and I cannot spot him.
23.10: But he knows the path I walk; when he has tried me, I will come out as gold.
23.11: My foot has stayed on his track; I have held to his path without turning off.
23.12: I have not departed from his commands; I have valued the words of his mouth more than my daily bread.
23.13: But he is resolute in his purpose, and who can change him? What he wills, that he carries out.
23.14: He will carry out what he has ordained for me; and there is much more still with him.
23.15: That is why I am shaken in his presence; when I think of him, dread fills me.
23.16: God has made my heart weak; the Almighty has shaken me with dread;
23.17: yet I am not cut off by the darkness, nor by the deep darkness covering my face.

24.1: Why does the Almighty not set appointed times, so that those who know him can witness his days of reckoning?
24.2: Some people move the boundary stones; they steal flocks by force and pasture them.
24.3: They drive away the orphan's donkey; they seize the widow's ox as collateral.
24.4: They shove the needy off the road; the poor of the land have all gone into hiding.
24.5: Look — like wild donkeys in the desert they go out to work, foraging in the wasteland for food for their children.
24.6: They harvest crops from fields that are not theirs; they glean leftover grapes in the wicked man's vineyard.
24.7: They spend the night naked, with no garment to cover them against the cold.
24.8: They are soaked by the mountain rains and press against the rock for any protection.
24.9: They snatch the fatherless infant from its mother's breast; they take collateral from the destitute.
24.10: The poor go about without clothing; starving, they carry the harvest sheaves.
24.11: They press olives among the terrace walls; they tread the winepresses, yet they go thirsty.
24.12: From the city the dying groan; the mortally wounded cry out — yet God lays no charge of wrongdoing.
24.13: These are among those who rebel against the light; they do not know its ways and will not walk its paths.
24.14: The murderer rises before dawn and kills the poor and needy; in the night he becomes the thief.
24.15: The adulterer's eye watches for the dusk, thinking, 'No one will spot me,' and he disguises his face.
24.16: In the darkness they break through walls; by day they stay shut away — they want nothing to do with the light.
24.17: The shadow of death is their morning; they are familiar with the horrors of death's darkness.
24.18: He skims across the surface of the water like foam; his share of the land is under a curse; he does not go near the vineyards.
24.19: Just as drought and heat evaporate the melting snow, so Sheol takes those who have sinned.
24.20: The womb that bore him forgets him; the worm devours him eagerly; he is no longer remembered; wickedness is broken off like a tree.
24.21: He preys on the childless woman who cannot conceive, and shows no kindness to the widow.
24.22: By his power he brings even the strong man down; he rises up, and no one's life is secure.
24.23: God allows him to rest in security, and he relies on that security — yet God's eyes are on every path he takes.
24.24: They are lifted up for a moment and then they are gone; they sink down and are gathered in with everyone else; they are cut off like the head of a stalk of grain.
24.25: And if this is not true, who will prove me wrong and show that my words are worthless?

29.1: Job continued his discourse and said:
29.2: Oh that I were as in the months gone by, in the days when God watched over me!
29.3: when his lamp shone on my head and by his light I walked through darkness;
29.4: as I was in the prime of life, when the friendship of God rested upon my tent;
29.5: when the Almighty was still with me, and my children were gathered around me;
29.6: when I bathed my steps in cream and the rock poured out streams of oil for me;
29.7: when I went out to the city gate and took my seat in the public square,
29.8: the young men saw me and stepped aside; the aged rose and stood to their feet.
29.9: the princes stopped speaking and covered their mouths with their hands;
29.10: the voice of the nobles fell silent; their tongues stuck to the roof of their mouths.
29.11: For the ear that heard me called me blessed, and the eye that saw me testified for me;
29.12: for I rescued the poor when they cried for help, and the fatherless who had no one to help them.
29.13: The blessing of the one about to perish came to me, and I made the widow's heart sing for joy.
29.14: I put on righteousness and it clothed me; my justice was like a robe and a turban.
29.15: I was eyes for the blind and feet for the lame.
29.16: I was a father to the needy, and I investigated the case of the one I did not know.
29.17: I broke the fangs of the wicked and forced the prey from his teeth.
29.18: Then I said, 'I shall die in my own nest and multiply my days like the sand.'
29.19: My roots spread out to the waters, and the dew rested all night on my branches;
29.20: my glory remained fresh within me, and my bow was always renewed in my hand.
29.21: Men listened to me; they waited and held their silence for my counsel.
29.22: After my words they spoke no more; my speech fell upon them.
29.23: They waited for me as for the rain and opened their mouths wide as for the spring rain.
29.24: When I smiled at them they could hardly believe it; they did not let the light of my face fall.
29.25: I chose the course for them and sat as their head; I lived as a king among his troops, as one who comforts those who mourn.

30.1: But now those younger than I hold me in contempt — men whose fathers I would have refused to place with the dogs of my flock.
30.2: Indeed, what use is the strength of their hands to me — men whose vigor has already perished?
30.3: Through want and famine they were barren; they fled into the desolate and waste wilderness.
30.4: They gathered saltbush leaves from among the shrubs; the root of the broom plant was their food.
30.5: They were driven out from human society; people shouted after them as after a thief.
30.6: They lived in gullies of the ravines, in holes of the earth, and among the rocks.
30.7: Among the bushes they cried out; beneath the nettles they gathered.
30.8: Children of fools, children of men without a name, they were flogged out of the land.
30.9: And now I have become their song and their byword.
30.10: They despise me and keep their distance; they do not hesitate to spit in my face.
30.11: Because God has loosed my cord and afflicted me, they have cast aside all restraint in my presence.
30.12: On my right the mob rises up; they push aside my feet and build against me their roads of ruin.
30.13: They wreck my path; they advance my downfall; they need no one to help them.
30.14: They come in as through a wide breach; they rush in amid the ruin.
30.15: Terrors are unleashed against me; my dignity is driven away like the wind, and my prosperity passes away like a cloud.
30.16: And now my soul is poured out within me; days of affliction have seized me.
30.17: At night my bones are racked with pain, and the pain that gnaws at me never rests.
30.18: With great force my clothing is seized by my disease; it binds me at the neck like the collar of my coat.
30.19: He has thrown me into the mud; I have become like dust and ashes.
30.20: I cry to you but you do not answer; I stand, and you only stare at me.
30.21: You have become cruel to me; with the power of your hand you oppose me.
30.22: You lift me up on the wind; you cause me to ride it and dissolve my substance.
30.23: For I know you will bring me to death, to the house appointed for all the living.
30.24: Yet does not one in a heap of ruins reach out his hand? Does one not cry out in his disaster?
30.25: Did I not weep for those in trouble? Was not my soul grieved for the poor?
30.26: But when I looked for good, evil came; and when I waited for light, darkness came instead.
30.27: My inner parts churn and do not rest; days of affliction have overtaken me.
30.28: I walk in mourning without the sun; I stand up in the assembly and cry out.
30.29: I have become a brother to jackals and a companion to wild birds.
30.30: My skin has turned black and peels away; my bones burn with fever.
30.31: My harp has been turned to mourning and my flute to the sound of weeping.

31.1: I made a covenant with my eyes; how then could I look upon a young woman?
31.2: For what is the portion God assigns from above, the heritage the Almighty deals from on high?
31.3: Is there not ruin for the unrighteous and disaster for those who work iniquity?
31.4: Does he not see my ways and number all my steps?
31.5: If I have walked in falsehood, or my foot has rushed toward deceit —
31.6: Let me be weighed in an honest balance, so that God may know my integrity.
31.7: If my step has turned from the right path, or my heart has followed my eyes, or any stain has clung to my hands —
31.8: Then let me plant and another harvest, and let my offspring be uprooted.
31.9: If my heart has been drawn away by a woman, or I have lurked at my neighbour's door —
31.10: Then let my wife grind for another man, and let others lie with her.
31.11: For that would be a shameful outrage and an iniquity deserving judgment.
31.12: For it is a fire that burns all the way to Abaddon and would consume all my harvest.
31.13: If I have dismissed the complaint of my servant or my maidservant when they had a dispute with me —
31.14: What could I do when God rises up? When he holds inquiry, what shall I answer him?
31.15: Did not the one who made me in the womb make him too? Did not one God form us both in the womb?
31.16: If I have refused the poor what they needed, or caused the widow's eyes to fail from waiting —
31.17: Or if I ate my food alone and the fatherless ate nothing —
31.18: For from my youth the fatherless grew up with me as with a father, and from birth I cared for the widow.
31.19: If I have seen anyone dying from lack of clothing, or any poor person without covering —
31.20: If he has not blessed me from his very body, and if the wool of my sheep has not kept him warm —
31.21: If I have raised my hand against the fatherless when I had allies at the city gate —
31.22: Then let my arm fall from its socket and my arm be broken from the elbow.
31.23: For the destruction God sends is a terror to me; before his majesty I cannot hold my ground.
31.24: If I have made gold my hope or called pure gold my security —
31.25: If I rejoiced that my wealth was great or that my hand had gained much —
31.26: If I gazed at the sun in its brilliance or the moon as it moved in splendor —
31.27: And my heart was secretly seduced, and my hand was kissed toward them —
31.28: This too would be an iniquity deserving judgment, for I would have denied the God above.
31.29: If I rejoiced when my enemy was ruined, or exulted when disaster found him —
31.30: I have not let my mouth sin by calling down a curse on his life —
31.31: If the men of my household have not said, 'Who has not been filled with his food?' —
31.32: No stranger had to sleep in the street; I opened my doors to the traveller.
31.33: If I have hidden my transgressions like Adam, concealing my iniquity in my heart —
31.34: Because I feared the crowd and the contempt of families terrified me, and so I kept silent and did not go out the door —
31.35: O that someone would hear me! Here is my signature — let the Almighty answer me! O that my accuser had written out his charges!
31.36: Surely I would carry it on my shoulder and wear it as a crown on my head.
31.37: I would give him an account of all my steps; I would approach him like a prince.
31.38: If my land has cried out against me and its furrows have wept —
31.39: If I have eaten its produce without paying, or stripped the life from its owners —
31.40: Then let thistles grow instead of wheat and weeds instead of barley. The words of Job are ended.

32.1: So these three men stopped answering Job, because he was righteous in his own eyes.
32.2: Then the anger of Elihu son of Barachel the Buzite, of the clan of Ram, burned — against Job, because he declared himself righteous rather than God.
32.3: He was also angry with his three friends because they had found no answer and yet had condemned Job.
32.4: Elihu had waited to speak with Job because the others were older than he was.
32.5: When Elihu saw that the three men had no answer, his anger was aroused.
32.6: Then Elihu son of Barachel the Buzite answered: I am young and you are old; that is why I was afraid and held back from telling you what I think.
32.7: I thought, 'Let the aged speak; let many years teach wisdom.'
32.8: But it is the Spirit in a person — the breath of the Almighty — that gives them understanding.
32.9: It is not the great who are always wise, nor the aged who always understand what is right.
32.10: Therefore I say: Listen to me; I too will express what I think.
32.11: I waited for your words; I listened carefully to your arguments while you searched for what to say.
32.12: I listened carefully to all of you, and not one of you has refuted Job or answered what he said.
32.13: Do not say, 'We have found wisdom; let God deal with him, not us.'
32.14: He has not directed his argument against me, and I will not reply to him with your kind of arguments.
32.15: They are astonished; they say nothing more; words have left them.
32.16: I waited, but they did not speak; they stood there and answered no more.
32.17: Then I said, 'I too will give my answer; I too will state what I think.'
32.18: For I am full of words; the Spirit within me urges me on.
32.19: My belly is like wine with no outlet — like new wineskins about to burst.
32.20: I will speak and find relief; I will open my lips and answer.
32.21: I will show partiality to no one, nor will I flatter anyone.
32.22: For I do not know how to flatter; if I did, my Maker would soon remove me.

33.1: But now, Job, hear my words and listen to everything I have to say.
33.2: See now, I have opened my mouth; my tongue speaks within my mouth.
33.3: My words come from an upright heart; my lips speak what I know sincerely.
33.4: The Spirit of God has made me, and the breath of the Almighty gives me life.
33.5: If you can answer me, set your words in order before me and take your stand.
33.6: See, I stand before God on your behalf as you wished; I too was formed from clay.
33.7: My dread will not terrify you, nor will my pressure be heavy upon you.
33.8: You have spoken in my hearing, and I have heard the words you said:
33.9: You said: 'I am clean, without transgression; I am innocent and there is no iniquity in me.'
33.10: But you also said: 'He finds grievances against me; he regards me as his enemy.'
33.11: He puts my feet in stocks; he watches all my paths.
33.12: But in this I tell you plainly that you are wrong: God is greater than any human being.
33.13: Why do you argue against him? For he does not give account for any of his actions.
33.14: For God speaks in one way and then another, though people do not notice.
33.15: In a dream, in a nighttime vision, when deep sleep falls on people as they slumber on their beds —
33.16: He opens people's ears and confirms what he is teaching them —
33.17: To turn them from their intended course and shield them from pride —
33.18: He keeps him from the pit and his life from crossing into death.
33.19: He is also chastened with pain upon his bed, and with unending conflict in his bones —
33.20: So that he cannot eat bread and his appetite revolts against any food.
33.21: His flesh wastes away until it is gone, and his bones that were hidden now protrude.
33.22: His life draws near the pit and his soul to the agents of death.
33.23: But if there is a messenger for him, a mediator — one in a thousand — who declares to the person what is right for them —
33.24: Then God is gracious to him and says: Spare him from going down to the pit; I have found a ransom.
33.25: His flesh becomes fresher than a child's; he is restored to the days of his youth.
33.26: He prays to God and God accepts him; he sees his face with a shout of joy, and God restores his righteousness.
33.27: He looks on people, and if any say: 'I sinned and bent what was right, and it brought me nothing good' —
33.28: God will deliver his life from the pit, and his life will see the light.
33.29: See, God does all these things — again and again — with a person —
33.30: To bring his life back from the pit, that he might be lit by the light of the living.
33.31: Pay close attention, Job; listen to me; be silent and I will speak.
33.32: If you have anything to say, answer me; speak up, for I want to see you vindicated.
33.33: If not, listen to me; be silent, and I will teach you wisdom.

40.1: Then the LORD answered Job and said:
40.2: Will a critic argue with the Almighty? Let the one who accuses God answer him.
40.4: I am of little account; what shall I say to you? I put my hand over my mouth.
40.5: I spoke once, but I will not answer again; twice, but I will add nothing more.
40.6: Then the LORD answered Job out of the storm:
40.7: Brace yourself like a warrior; I will question you, and you will answer me.
40.8: Would you discredit my justice? Would you condemn me so that you can be righteous?
40.9: Do you have an arm like God? Can you thunder with a voice like his?
40.10: Adorn yourself with majesty and dignity; dress yourself in glory and splendor.
40.11: Unleash the fury of your wrath; look at everyone who is proud and humiliate him.
40.12: Look at every proud person and humble him; trample the wicked where they stand.
40.13: Thrust them into the dust together; bind their faces in the realm of concealment.
40.14: Then I myself will acknowledge that your own right hand can give you victory.
40.15: Look at Behemoth, which I made just as I made you; it eats grass like an ox.
40.16: Look at the strength in his haunches, the power in the muscles of his belly.
40.17: He swings his tail like a cedar; the sinews of his thighs are tightly woven.
40.18: His bones are like bronze pipes; his limbs are like bars of iron.
40.19: He ranks first among the works of God; only his Maker can approach him with a sword.
40.20: The mountains produce food for him; all the wild beasts of the field play around him.
40.21: He rests beneath the lotus plants, sheltered in the reeds and swamps.
40.22: The trees shade him with their branches; the willows of the brook encircle him.
40.23: Even if the river floods, Behemoth is untroubled; he is at ease though the Jordan rushes into his mouth.
40.24: Can anyone catch him while he watches? Can anyone trap him by piercing his nose?

41.1: Can you pull Leviathan up with a hook, or hold down his tongue with a rope?
41.2: Can you put a reed through his nose or run a hook through his jaw?
41.3: Will he beg you for mercy? Will he address you with soft words?
41.4: Will he make an agreement with you? Will you bind him as your permanent slave?
41.5: Will you play with him as you would a pet bird? Will you put him on a leash for your young women?
41.6: Will trading partners negotiate for him? Will merchants distribute him among buyers?
41.7: Can you pierce his skin with harpoons, or his head with fishing lances?
41.8: Try touching him; you will remember the encounter and never do it again.
41.9: Any hope of mastering him is an illusion; even the sight of him knocks a man down.
41.10: No one is fierce enough to rouse him; who then is able to stand before me?
41.11: Who has given me anything that I should owe him in return? Everything under all the heavens belongs to me.
41.12: I will not be silent about his structure, his power, or the beauty of his proportion.
41.13: Who can remove the outer layer of his skin? Who can break through his double layer of defense?
41.14: Who can pry open the doors of his mouth? His teeth are ringed with dread.
41.15: His back is like rows of shields, locked together like a tight seal.
41.16: Each scale is pressed so close to the next that no breath of air can pass between.
41.17: They cling together one to another; they lock onto each other and will not come apart.
41.18: His sneezes send out flashes of light; his eyes shine like the gleam of dawn.
41.19: Burning torches leap from his mouth; sparks of fire fly from it.
41.20: Smoke billows from his nostrils like steam from a boiling pot over burning rushes.
41.21: His breath sets coals on fire; a flame shoots out of his mouth.
41.22: Strength resides in his neck; dismay dances ahead of him.
41.23: The layers of his flesh are locked together; they are hard on him and immovable.
41.24: His heart is like a rock, as hard as the lower millstone.
41.25: When he rears up, the mighty are terrified; they fall back before his thrashing.
41.26: The sword that strikes him cannot hold; neither spear, dart, nor javelin.
41.27: Iron is nothing to him but straw; bronze is like rotten wood.
41.28: The arrow cannot make him run; sling stones are as light as chaff to him.
41.29: He regards war clubs as straw; he laughs at the sound of a shaken lance.
41.30: His underbelly is like sharp shards of pottery; he plows jagged furrows through the mud.
41.31: He makes the depths churn like a boiling pot; he churns the sea like a jar of perfume.
41.32: A glistening path shines behind him; one would think the sea has turned white.
41.33: There is nothing on earth to compare with him; he is a creature fashioned without fear.
41.34: He surveys everything that exalts itself; he is king over all the proud.

42.2: I know now that you can do all things and that no plan of yours can be stopped.
42.3: You asked: 'Who is this who speaks without knowledge?' Therefore I have spoken of things I did not understand — things too wonderful for me that I did not know.
42.4: You said: 'Listen, and I will speak; I will question you, and you shall answer me.'
42.5: I had heard about you by report, but now my own eyes have seen you.
42.6: Therefore I take back my words and repent in dust and ashes.
42.7: After the LORD had finished speaking with Job, he said to Eliphaz the Temanite: My anger burns against you and your two friends, because you have not spoken the truth about me, as my servant Job has.
42.8: Now take seven bulls and seven rams, go to my servant Job, and offer a burnt offering for yourselves.
