My servant Job will pray for you, and I will accept his prayer on your behalf, so that I do not deal with you as your foolishness deserves — for you have not spoken about me what is true, as my servant Job has.
42.9: Eliphaz the Temanite, Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite went and did what the LORD told them to do, and the LORD accepted Job's prayer.
42.10: The LORD restored Job's fortunes after he prayed for his friends; the LORD doubled everything Job had previously.
42.11: Then all his brothers and sisters and everyone who had known him before came and ate a meal with him in his house. They consoled him and comforted him for all the trouble the LORD had brought upon him. Each one gave him a piece of silver and a gold ring.
42.12: The LORD blessed the latter part of Job's life more than the former: fourteen thousand sheep, six thousand camels, a thousand teams of oxen, and a thousand female donkeys.
42.13: He also had seven sons and three daughters.
42.14: He named the first Jemimah, the second Keziah, and the third Keren-happuch.
42.15: In all the land no women were as beautiful as Job's daughters, and their father gave them property alongside their brothers.
42.16: After all this, Job lived a hundred and forty years and saw his children and grandchildren to the fourth generation.
42.17: So Job died, old and satisfied with life.

34.2: Hear my words, O wise men; listen to me, you who have knowledge.
34.3: For the ear tests words as the palate tastes food.
34.4: Let us choose together what is right; let us determine among ourselves what is good.
34.5: For Job has said, 'I am righteous, and God has denied me my right.'
34.6: Must I lie about my case? My wound is past healing, though I have done no wrong.
34.7: What man is like Job, who drinks mockery like water?
34.8: Who keeps company with evildoers and walks with wicked men.
34.9: For he has said, 'A man gains nothing by delighting in God.'
34.10: Therefore, men of understanding, listen to me: far be it from God to do evil, far from the Almighty to commit wrong.
34.11: For he repays each person according to their work and deals with everyone according to their conduct.
34.12: Of a truth, God will not act wickedly; the Almighty will not pervert justice.
34.13: Who gave him his charge over the earth? Who appointed him over the whole world?
34.14: If he should set his heart against us and gather to himself his Spirit and breath —
34.15: All flesh would perish together, and human beings would return to dust.
34.16: If you have understanding, hear this; listen to what I am saying.
34.17: Shall one who hates justice govern? Will you condemn the One who is righteous and mighty?
34.18: Is it right to say to a king, 'You are worthless,' and to nobles, 'You are wicked'?
34.19: How much less to him who shows no partiality to princes and does not favor the rich over the poor, for they are all the work of his hands?
34.20: In a moment they die; at midnight the people are convulsed and pass away, and the mighty are removed — without human effort.
34.21: For his eyes are on the ways of everyone, and he sees all their steps.
34.22: There is no darkness or deep shadow where evildoers can hide themselves.
34.23: For he does not impose on anyone more than is warranted to bring them before God in judgment.
34.24: He shatters the mighty without investigation and sets others in their place.
34.25: For he knows their deeds, and he overturns them in the night; they are crushed.
34.26: He strikes them for their wickedness in an open place where others can see —
34.27: Because they turned away from following him and gave no thought to any of his ways —
34.28: So that they brought the cry of the poor before him, and he heard the outcry of the afflicted.
34.29: When he is quiet, who can condemn? When he hides his face, who can see him? Whether dealing with a nation or with a single person —
34.30: lest a godless person reign and ensnare the people.
34.31: Has anyone said to God, 'I have endured my punishment; I will not sin any more'?
34.32: And: 'Teach me what I cannot see; if I have done wrong, I will not do it again.'
34.33: Should he repay you on your own terms because you reject his? For you must choose, not I; tell me then what you know.
34.34: People of understanding will agree with me, and every wise person who hears me will say:
34.35: 'Job speaks without knowledge; his words are without insight.'
34.36: Would that Job were tested to the limit, because his answers are like those of wicked men.
34.37: For he adds rebellion to his sin; he mocks us and heaps up words against God.

35.2: Do you think this is just when you say, 'My righteousness is more than God's'?
35.3: For you say, 'What does it benefit you? What do I gain from not sinning?'
35.4: I will give you an answer, and your companions along with you.
35.5: Look up at the sky and see; behold the clouds, higher than you.
35.6: If you sin, what do you accomplish against him? If your transgressions multiply, what do you do to him?
35.7: If you are righteous, what do you give him? What does he receive from your hand?
35.8: Your wickedness harms only someone like yourself, and your righteousness benefits only other people.
35.9: Under the weight of great oppression people cry out; they call for help from the arm of the powerful.
35.10: But no one asks, 'Where is God my Maker, who gives songs in the night?'
35.11: Who teaches us more than the animals of the earth and makes us wiser than the birds of the sky?
35.12: There they cry, but he does not answer, because of the pride of evil people.
35.13: Certainly God does not hear a hollow cry; the Almighty does not regard it.
35.14: How much less when you say you cannot see him, that the case is before him, and you must wait for him —
35.15: But now, because God has not punished in his anger and does not take sharp notice of every transgression —
35.16: So Job opens his mouth with empty words; he heaps up words without knowledge.

36.2: Bear with me a little longer and I will show you, for I still have something to say in God's defense.
36.3: I will draw my knowledge from a wide range and ascribe righteousness to my Maker.
36.4: For truly my words are not false; one complete in knowledge stands before you.
36.5: Behold, God is mighty and despises no one; he is mighty in strength of understanding.
36.6: He does not keep the wicked alive, but gives justice to the poor.
36.7: He does not take his eyes off the righteous; he seats them on the throne with kings and exalts them forever.
36.8: And if they are bound in chains, caught in ropes of affliction —
36.9: Then he shows them what they have done and their transgressions — that they have acted arrogantly.
36.10: He opens their ears to correction and commands that they turn from evil.
36.11: If they listen and serve him, they will complete their days in prosperity and their years in comfort.
36.12: But if they do not listen, they will perish by the sword and die without ever understanding.
36.13: The godless in heart store up resentment; they do not cry to God when he binds them.
36.14: They die young, and their life ends among the cult prostitutes.
36.15: But he rescues the afflicted person through their suffering and opens their ears through oppression.
36.16: Indeed, he was drawing you from the jaws of distress into a broad place, free from pressure, where your table would be spread with rich food.
36.17: But now you are full of the judgment due the wicked; judgment and justice have seized you.
36.18: Beware that anger does not lead you into scoffing; do not let the greatness of the required payment turn you away.
36.19: Will your wealth hold you in distress, or all the forces of your strength?
36.20: Do not long for the night, when people vanish from where they stand.
36.21: Be careful not to turn toward evil, for this is what you have been choosing over enduring affliction.
36.22: Behold, God is exalted in his power; who is a teacher like him?
36.23: Who has prescribed his path for him? Who can say, 'You have done wrong'?
36.24: Remember to extol his work, which people have sung about through the ages.
36.25: All people have seen it; everyone looks at it from a distance.
36.26: Behold, God is great, beyond our knowing; the number of his years cannot be counted.
36.27: For he draws up the drops of water; they distill as rain from the mist —
36.28: which the clouds pour down and drop on people in abundance.
36.29: Can anyone understand the spreading of the clouds or the thunder from his canopy?
36.30: See, he scatters his lightning around him and covers the depths of the sea.
36.31: For by these he governs peoples and provides food in abundance.
36.32: He covers his hands with the lightning and commands it to strike its mark.
36.33: Its thunder announces his presence; the cattle also sense the coming storm.

37.1: At this my heart trembles and leaps from its place.
37.2: Listen carefully to the roar of his voice and the rumble that goes out from his mouth.
37.3: He sends it out under the whole sky, and his lightning to the ends of the earth.
37.4: After the lightning a voice roars; he thunders in his majestic voice and does not hold the lightnings back once his voice sounds.
37.5: God thunders astoundingly with his voice; he does great things beyond our understanding.
37.6: For he says to the snow: 'Fall upon the earth,' and to the gentle rain and to the great downpour of his might.
37.7: He seals the hand of every person so that all humanity may perceive his work.
37.8: Then the wild animals go into their dens and stay in their lairs.
37.9: From the south comes the whirlwind and from the north comes the cold.
37.10: By the breath of God ice is formed and the wide waters are frozen solid.
37.11: He loads the clouds with moisture and scatters his lightning-cloud.
37.12: It turns in every direction by his guidance, to do whatever he commands across the face of the inhabited earth.
37.13: He brings it whether as discipline, for his land's sake, or as steadfast love.
37.14: Listen to this, Job; stop and reflect on the wondrous works of God.
37.15: Do you know how God arranged these things and caused the lightning in his cloud to flash?
37.16: Do you understand how the clouds are balanced — the wonders of the one who is perfect in knowledge?
37.17: Why your clothes are warm when the earth lies still under the south wind?
37.18: Have you joined him in spreading out the sky, firm and hard as a poured metal mirror?
37.19: Tell us what we should say to him — we cannot arrange our words because of our darkness.
37.20: Should he be told that I want to speak? Can anyone speak to him without being consumed?
37.21: And now people cannot look at the bright light in the clouds; but the wind comes and sweeps them away.
37.22: From the north comes golden brightness; God comes in fearsome majesty.
37.23: As for the Almighty, we cannot reach him; he is great in power, in justice, and in abundant righteousness; he does not oppress.
37.24: Therefore people fear him; he does not attend to those who are wise in their own eyes.

38.1: Then the LORD answered Job out of the whirlwind:
38.2: Who is this who clouds my purpose with words that have no knowledge behind them?
38.3: Brace yourself like a man; I will question you and you must answer me.
38.4: Where were you when I laid the earth's foundation? Tell me, if you have understanding.
38.5: Who determined its measurements — do you know? Who stretched the measuring line across it?
38.6: On what were its foundations set? Or who laid its cornerstone —
38.7: When the morning stars rang out together and all the divine beings shouted for joy?
38.8: Who shut in the sea with doors when it burst out from the womb?
38.9: When I made clouds its garment and thick darkness its swaddling cloth —
38.10: When I fixed its boundaries and set its bars and doors —
38.11: When I said: 'This far you may come, and no farther; here your proud waves will stop.'
38.12: Have you ever commanded the morning since your life began, or told the dawn where to take its place?
38.13: So that it would seize the earth's edges and shake the wicked out of it?
38.14: The earth changes like clay under a seal; its features stand out like a garment.
38.15: From the wicked their light is taken away, and the arm raised in violence shall be broken.
38.16: Have you gone down to the springs of the sea, or walked through the depths of the abyss?
38.17: Have the gates of death been revealed to you? Have you seen the doors of deepest darkness?
38.18: Have you comprehended the earth's full breadth? Tell me, if you know all of it.
38.19: Where is the way to where light lives? And where does darkness make its home?
38.20: Can you take it to its territory and find the paths to its dwelling?
38.21: You know, of course — for you were born then, and the number of your days is very great!
38.22: Have you gone into the storehouses of the snow, or have you seen the storehouses of the hail —
38.23: Which I have kept in reserve for the time of trouble, for the day of battle and war?
38.24: By what path does the light divide, or the east wind scatter itself across the earth?
38.25: Who has cut a channel for the flood of waters, or a path for the thunderbolt?
38.26: To bring rain on a land where no person lives, on the desert with no human in it —
38.27: To satisfy the desolate wasteland and make the tender grass spring up?
38.28: Does the rain have a father? Who has fathered the drops of dew?
38.29: From whose womb did the ice come? Who gave birth to the frost of heaven?
38.30: The waters harden like stone, and the surface of the deep is locked.
38.31: Can you bind the chains of the Pleiades, or can you release the belt of Orion?
38.32: Can you bring out Mazzaroth in its season? Can you guide the Bear with its cubs?
38.33: Do you know the laws of heaven? Can you establish its rule over the earth?
38.34: Can you raise your voice to the clouds and make a flood of water come down?
38.35: Can you send out lightning bolts on commission, and do they report back to you: 'Here we are'?
38.36: Who has placed wisdom within the hidden self, or given understanding to the mind?
38.37: Who has the wisdom to count the clouds? Who can tilt the water jars of heaven —
38.38: When the dust hardens into a mass and the clods of dirt cling together?
38.39: Do you hunt prey for the lion and fill the hunger of its cubs —
38.40: When they crouch in their dens and wait in ambush in their thickets?
38.41: Who provides the raven its food when its chicks cry out to God and wander about hungry?

39.1: Do you know when the mountain goats give birth? Can you watch over the birth of the does?
39.2: Can you count the months they carry young to term, and do you know when they give birth?
39.3: They crouch down and deliver their young; they are released from their birth pangs.
39.4: Their young grow strong and thrive in the open; they go out and do not return to their mothers.
39.5: Who set the wild donkey free? Who opened the bonds of the onager?
39.6: I have made the wilderness its home, the salt land its dwelling.
39.7: It mocks the city's noise and does not hear the driver's shout.
39.8: The mountain range is its pasture, and it searches out every bit of green growth.
39.9: Will the wild ox agree to serve you, or spend the night at your feeding trough?
39.10: Can you harness the wild ox to plow your furrow, or will it follow you to break up the valley soil?
39.11: Will you rely on it because it is so strong? Will you leave your hard work to it?
39.12: Will you count on it to bring in your grain and gather it to your threshing floor?
39.13: Did you give the ostrich its proud wings, or the stork its feathers?
39.14: She lays her eggs in the dirt and lets the sand warm them —
39.15: Forgetting that a foot might trample them or a wild animal crush them.
39.16: She treats her chicks as if they are not hers; she is indifferent to her fruitless toil.
39.17: For God has withheld wisdom from her and given her no share of understanding.
39.18: But when she raises herself to run, she laughs at the horse and its rider.
39.19: Did you give the horse its might? Did you clothe its neck with a flowing mane?
39.20: Can you make it leap like a locust? Its majestic snorting is terrifying.
39.21: It paws the ground in the valley and surges forward in its strength; it charges into the battle line.
39.22: It laughs at fear and is not alarmed; it does not draw back from the sword.
39.23: The quiver rattles on its flank, along with the flashing spear and javelin.
39.24: With fierce excitement it devours the ground; at the sound of the trumpet it cannot stand still.
39.25: At each trumpet blast it cries out 'Aha!' It scents the battle from far away — the roar of commanders and the war cry.
39.26: Does the hawk soar by your wisdom and spread its wings toward the south?
39.27: Does the eagle soar at your command and build its nest on the heights?
39.28: It lives on the cliff and stays there, on the rocky crag and the natural stronghold.
39.29: From there it watches for food; its eyes spot prey from far away.
39.30: Its young ones drink blood; where the slain are, there the eagle is.
